story:
No one passing the patrol car would have guessed why Terry was in the back seat. He’d been arrested less than an hour ago, on suspicion of Lydia’s murder, and the officer who’d cuffed him had said only that there was enough to bring him in. At a red light the officer asked if he was cold, then drifted into small talk about the winter. Terry tuned him out. His thoughts kept circling: prison for something he didn’t do; Michelle and their baby he might never meet; whispers at school about how much time he’d been spending with Lydia; a week to Christmas and no gifts bought. The ideas gusted through him like wind rattling an empty house.

When he tried to pinch the bridge of his nose, the handcuffs tugged him back. He dozed off and woke to the harsh lights of the station. The door flew open; he almost spilled out. Led down a stretched-out hallway to Interrogation Room #1, he took in the hideous blue walls pocked by fist-sized holes and a lone window framing falling snow. They uncuffed him only to re-cuff him to the table. His wrists stung. To anchor himself, he counted cracks in the ceiling and muttered about taxpayer money.

A tall man without a uniform entered, all shadow and authority. He set down a wallet, a badge gleaming with the name Robert, keys, a worn leather briefcase, and a pack of cigarettes. He offered one and, when Terry didn’t answer, said he didn’t smoke either—anymore. He reminisced about his first drag, how it felt like a pair of hands closing around his throat and then releasing, air flooding back. Then he let the image hang there. Terry stared at the floor. Robert told him the sooner he talked, the sooner this could be sorted. “I don’t have anything to say,” Terry whispered.

The briefcase snapped open. Files spread across the metal. Robert recited the outline: an eighteen-year-old girl, strangled. He paused, held Terry’s gaze. She was three weeks pregnant. The words froze the room. Lydia—pregnant? She was the kind of student teachers bragged about: straight A’s, clubs, charity, no time for trouble. Terry rasped that he hadn’t hurt her; yes, it was a tragedy; yes, God rest her soul; she’d been a bright kid. Robert pounced. Do you take bright kids home after school? Photos slid out—Terry’s own Ford Explorer, the same one he and Michelle had driven back from their anniversary. In the pictures, Terry and Lydia can be seen embracing.

Heat rose under Terry’s collar. He should never have crossed that line. Whatever the intention, whatever the circumstances, a teacher has no business being that close to a student. He pictured Michelle, her belly under a winter coat, the nursery still half-painted. He pictured the rumors, a web tightening around him. Robert kept watching, weighing, waiting. The smoking talk hadn’t been small talk. It was a metaphor laid like a snare: hands on a throat, then release. Terry could feel the room closing in, the cuffs biting, the snow outside falling in quiet judgment.

He swallowed. Until the photos, some stubborn part of him had still believed this was a misunderstanding that could be cleared up. Now he saw the shape of the case they meant to build. “I’d like to speak to my attorney,” he said.
